mnemonics.title: Complexity Ranking Order
mnemonics.content: For exam: Remember 'CLaNQPFX' - Constant, Logarithmic, Linear, n log n, Quadratic, Polynomial (n^k), Factorial/Exponential. This is from fastest to slowest.

mnemonics.title: Master Theorem Cases
mnemonics.content: Case 1: 'Far below' - polyless (n^log_b a / f(n) → ∞). Case 2: 'Same level' - equal (n^log_b a / f(n) → c). Case 3: 'Far above' - polygreat (n^log_b a / f(n) → 0). Think: 'FAr POLYgone = FAR + POLY'.

mnemonics.title: Big O Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: Big O = Upper (O for 'Over' or 'Ceiling'). Big Omega = Lower (Ω looks like 'U' flipped - Under/lower bound). Big Theta = Same from both sides (symmetric).

acronyms.expansion: Time complexity as function of input size n
acronyms.usage: Used to express running time of algorithms. Example: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n means algorithm divides into 2 subproblems of half size with n work at each level.
When we say Θ(n), we mean the running time is both O(n) and Ω(n), meaning it grows exactly linearly.
When we say Ω(n), we mean the algorithm takes at least linear time in the best case.

visualTips.visualization: Picture a graph with n on X-axis and time on Y-axis. O(1) is flat horizontal line. O(log n) is almost flat curve. O(n) is straight diagonal line at 45°. O(n²) is parabola. O(2^n) and O(n!) curve upward dramatically like a rocket launch.

Visualize a tree where root represents original problem of size n, each level splits into 'b' branches each of size n/b, and leaves represent base cases. Count levels (height = log_b n) and work per level to get total complexity.

Think of O(g(n)) as an upper envelope - the complexity curve stays below this envelope. Ω(g(n)) is a lower envelope - curve stays above. Θ(g(n)) means both envelopes touch the curve, tightly bounding it.

analogies.analogy: Big O is like the speed limit on a highway - it tells you the maximum speed you'll ever go, but you might go slower. O(100) means you'll never exceed 100 km/h, even if you drive at 60 km/h.

If n=10: O(1)=1 step, O(log n)=3 steps, O(n)=10 steps, O(n log n)=30 steps, O(n²)=100 steps. It's like the difference between finding a book in an organized library (log n) vs checking every book on every shelf (n).

Think of solving a puzzle: You have a pile of n pieces. You split it into b equal piles (a of them), solve each smaller pile, then combine results. Case 1: combining is cheap (polyless). Case 2: combining costs same as splitting. Case 3: combining is expensive (polygreat).
